ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee entry (FOODON_03315671); beef-specific congee is not individually coded. Closest parent match used — beef congee is a subcategory of congee.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers rice porridge/congee generically; no beef-specific congee code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for beef congee; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of beef porridge is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 360 kcal, 24 g protein, 12 g fat, and 36 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for rice congee with meat used as the primary reference. Values for beef congee are adapted from the closest HPB entry (congee with meat/fish) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef and rice porridge components. Sodium value of 240 mg/100g is consistent with soy-sauce-seasoned congee preparations documented in HPB data. All per_100g values are consistent with the input record and supported by this adapted match.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for beef congee. GI of 78 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice congee / rice porridge (GI ~78–81, mean 78), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Prolonged boiling of white rice during congee preparation gelatinises starch fully, producing a very high GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 36.0) / 100 = 28.1 for a 400 g bowl.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g bowl delivers approximately 960 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2,400 mg daily limit — and the low potassium content provides insufficient counter-balance; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce, avoid adding table salt, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~78) from fully gelatinised white rice starch with low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice congee has a GI of approximately 78 due to prolonged cooking fully breaking down starch granules; with only 0.4 g fibre per 100 g, glucose absorption is rapid — patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice congee, increase the beef-to-rice ratio to raise protein content, and monitor post-meal blood glucose carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (1.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.1 g/100g), and borderline sodium (240 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from beef, trace trans fat, and elevated sodium in a typical 400 g serving raises LDL-cholesterol and blood pressure risk; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose lean cuts of beef, and request preparation without added oil or fried garnishes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from red meat amplifies inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can amplify inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; consider substituting with fish or chicken, retaining ginger for its modest anti-inflammatory benefit, and avoiding fried shallot garnishes that generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Porridge (牛肉粥, Bubur Daging Lembu) is a smooth, slow-cooked rice congee dish popular across Singapore and the broader Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian food culture. It is prepared by simmering white rice in a large volume of water or stock for an extended period — typically 45 to 90 minutes — until the grains fully break down into a thick, silky porridge. Tender slices of beef (commonly flank, sirloin, or minced beef) are added toward the end of cooking or served raw and poached in the hot congee at the table. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, and white pepper, and garnished with spring onions, sesame oil, and sometimes fried shallots. Per 100 g, beef porridge provides approximately 90 kcal, 6 g protein, 3 g fat (1 g saturated), 9 g carbohydrate, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 240 mg sodium. The glycemic index is very high (~78) due to the complete gelatinisation of white rice starch during prolonged boiling, making it a high-glycemic-load meal at a standard 400 g serving (GL ~28). Clinically, it is a concern for type 2 diabetes patients due to rapid glucose absorption, and for hypertension patients due to sodium from soy sauce. It is a common breakfast and comfort food in Singapore hawker centres and is also consumed during illness or recovery. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to beef content, and contains soy allergens from soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef congee?
What is the GI of congee?
Is beef porridge high in sodium?
Is beef porridge good for hypertension?
How much protein in beef congee?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for congee with meat, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for beef and white rice. No exact beef porridge entry was found in a single approved source; values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (white rice congee, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for beef congee as a composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central composite dish entries as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation not reported in the primary adapted source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo adapted source entry as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 6.0, fat_g 3.0, carbohydrate_g 9.0) are non-zero and supported by the adapted HPB/USDA source match. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100g reflects trace trans fat from beef (naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid); this is a confirmed trace value consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for lean beef. Input cultural metadata contained inconsistencies (ethnic_tags included 'Noodle', 'Coffee', 'Cake' which are not relevant to beef porridge; country_of_origin listed as Singapore while primary_cuisine listed as Filipino — corrected to Singaporean/Chinese cuisine with Singapore as region of origin, as beef congee is a well-established Singaporean hawker dish of Chinese culinary heritage). Two duplicate portion entries with different gram weights (400g and 500g both marked is_default=true) were present in input; 400g selected as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited hawker bowl size. Halal status set to false as standard beef porridge preparations in Singapore may use non-halal soy sauce or pork-derived stock; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific vendor. Ontology match types corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the scoring model.
